rationale: Energy infrastructure reconstruction at baseline standard typically draws on concessional EBRD/EIB lending (primary tranche) and EU Facility grant support, with public-utility equity contribution. Private commercial finance rarely available at baseline in conflict-adjacent environment.
notes: RDNA3 §4 energy financing analysis; EBRD Ukraine Energy Sector Assessment 2023

rationale: Code-compliant energy reconstruction unlocks higher EU grant share (energy efficiency reform milestones in Ukraine Plan). Concessional tranche remains primary for large capital requirements.

Build-back-better energy projects (combined-cycle gas, renewables integration, grid modernisation) attract EU green-conditionality grants at premium rate. Private equity viable for commercially operating assets (e.g. DTEK wind/solar). MIGA War & Civil Disturbance covers private equity risk.

Healthcare reconstruction carries the highest grant-eligibility profile of any sector. EU Facility (Pillar IV social infrastructure), WHO, UNICEF, bilateral donors (UK, Germany, Netherlands) all prioritise healthcare. Concessional lending (EBRD Social Infrastructure, EIB Health) fills the gap.
notes: RDNA3 §6 social sector financing; bilateral donor commitments post-Okhmatdyt 2024

rationale: Code-compliant healthcare (energy efficiency, accessibility, seismic) unlocks EU structural-fund style grant streams. Higher grant share reflects regulatory mandate.

Build-back-better healthcare (microgrid, telemedicine, passive standard, modular clinical) commands highest grant share under EU green-conditionality. WHO Health Emergency Preparedness grants applicable. Private sector not applicable for public hospitals.

Education reconstruction primarily grant-funded (EU Facility Pillar IV, bilateral donors, UNICEF). Ministry of Education budget contributes public equity. Concessional World Bank or EBRD Social Sector lending fills remaining gap.
Code-compliant education (bomb shelter, energy efficiency, accessibility) qualifies for enhanced EU grant tranche.
Digital classroom, green building, and community learning hub add-ons unlock EU green grants and EdTech donor contributions.

Residential reconstruction involves largest ticket (8 districts targeted in 50-asset pipeline). EU Facility (Pillar III economic recovery) and bilateral housing grants anchor the stack. Municipal equity (land, existing foundations) large. Some private co-investment from returning resident/diaspora possible.
Energy-efficient housing reconstruction (passive house standard, heat pump) unlocks EU green grants. EBRD Green Cities applicable for district-scale projects.

Build-back-better housing (mass timber, passive standard, EV charging, district heating upgrade) attracts EU green-conditionality grants at higher rate. Some private co-investment for mixed-tenure development.

Heritage reconstruction is almost entirely grant-funded. EU Culture Reconstruction fund, Council of Europe, UNESCO, bilateral cultural donors (France, Italy, Germany, Poland) and national budget. Very limited scope for commercial finance in heritage assets.
notes: RDNA3 §Cultural Heritage; Council of Europe Kyiv Initiative

rationale: Code-compliant heritage (seismic, fire, accessibility improvements within conservation constraints) attracts enhanced EU grant. ICOMOS-compliant specification required.

Build-back-better heritage (sustainable energy systems within conservation envelope, digital interpretation, universal access) unlocks EU green-cultural intersection grants. Occupied-territory assets: all finance contingent on liberation; pipeline documentation only.

Transport infrastructure at baseline draws heavily on concessional EBRD/EIB lending (EBRD is the largest transport lender in Ukraine). EU TEN-T extension grants applicable to strategic corridors. Public equity from state transport enterprises (Ukrzaliznytsia, Ukravtodor, USPA).
Code-compliant transport (EU road safety standards, electrification-ready rail, green port requirements) unlocks higher EU grant under TEN-T.

Build-back-better transport (electrification, green port, hydrogen-ready logistics) attracts EU green-transport grants and some private PPP equity (port logistics, rail freight). MIGA covers private tranche against war risk.

Water and sanitation infrastructure reconstruction draws on EU Facility (WASH track), UNICEF, USAID, bilateral donors (Germany, Netherlands). Concessional EBRD and World Bank municipal infrastructure lending fills mid-tranche. Municipal Vodokanal equity.
Code-compliant water (EU drinking water directive standards, energy-efficient pumping) qualifies for enhanced EU grant and EBRD Green Cities facilities.
Build-back-better water (smart metering, energy recovery, circular biosolids) attracts EU green grants and EBRD Green Cities. No private sector applicable for public utilities.

Industrial reconstruction is primarily a commercial activity. State-owned enterprises (e.g. Metinvest, state steelworks) contribute equity; private operators (DTEK, agricultural holdings) bring private capital. Concessional EBRD/IFC lending bridges the risk gap. Grant share limited to strategic national-security assets only.
notes: RDNA3 §Industry; IFC Ukraine Private Sector Assessment 2023

rationale: Environmental compliance upgrade (EU taxonomy-aligned) attracts limited EU grant component. Private equity remains primary; MIGA covers against war risk.

Industrial decarbonisation (EAF steelmaking, green hydrogen DRI) qualifies for EU Just Transition and Innovation Fund grants. EBRD Green Economy Financing. Private equity viability depends on carbon price trajectory.
Public administration building reconstruction is primarily government-funded (UA State Budget, EU budget support) with EU Facility grant component (governance reform milestones).
Code-compliant government buildings (energy efficiency, accessibility, digital infrastructure) attract EU green-governance grants.
Build-back-better government buildings (passive standard, heat pump, digital services infrastructure) attract maximum EU green grant share.
